pattern: Whether an element qualifies depends only on its own occurrence count and how many other distinct values share that same count, so tallying value-frequencies and then frequency-of-frequencies in two linear passes answers the question without re-scanning for each candidate.
hints: First count how many times each distinct value occurs across the whole array -- a single hash map pass.
Then count how many distinct values share each frequency value -- a second hash map, this time keyed by the frequency itself.
Scan the original array left to right and return the first element whose own frequency maps to a count of exactly 1 in that second map; if the scan finishes with no match, return -1.

edge_inputs.description: statement example, two values tie at frequency 1 so neither qualifies, third value wins
statement example, first element already qualifies
statement example, both values share the same frequency so no answer exists
single element -- trivially unique frequency of 1
three distinct values all occurring once -- none unique since all three share frequency 1
all identical values -- the single distinct value trivially has a unique frequency
later element has a frequency unique on its own, but an earlier tied pair must be skipped first

stress.description: large random arrays over a bounded value range so frequency ties are common, exercising both hash passes at scale
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, size)
    value_range = max(1, n // 20)
    nums = [rng.randint(1, value_range) for _ in range(n)]
    return [nums]
